items.title: A social ecological approach to social contagion of self-harm among young people
items.author: Runsen Chen
items.content: Adolescents are particularly at risk for social contagion effects of self-harm. In this Review, Chen et al. specify factors that exacerbate self-harm social contagion at the individual, interpersonal, community and societal levels and discuss how to bridge psychological and public health perspectives to mitigate self-harm risk in this age group.

items.title: The multi-level social dilemmas of intergroup interactions
items.author: Qinyu Xiao
items.content: The multi-level social dilemmas of intergroup interactions

items.title: Bridging fair-aware artificial intelligence and co-creation for equitable mental healthcare
items.author: Theodora Chaspari
items.content: Machine learning algorithms can increase the effectiveness of mental health interventions, but biased systems might exacerbate current disparities in case identification and treatment. In this Perspective, Timmons et al. propose a model that integrates artificial intelligence methods with co-creation techniques to reduce bias and increase inclusion in mental healthcare.

items.title: Finding the optimal retention parameters for learning
items.author: Ewan Murray
items.content: Finding the optimal retention parameters for learning

items.title: Material perception connects vision, cognition and action
items.author: Chenxi Liao
items.content: Humans can infer a range of material properties from a brief glance. In this Review, Xiao and Liao discuss the varied facets of visual material perception, including its relationship to action planning and broader cognition.

items.title: How the brain constructs and uses meaning
items.author: Veronica Diveica
items.content: How the brain constructs and uses meaning

items.title: Inclusive writing in the social sciences
items.author: Teresa Roberts
items.content: Deficit-based narratives in social science use privileged identities as a reference, perpetuating societal biases that marginalize diverse communities. Researchers can promote a shift away from deficit-based narratives and support development of generalizable social-science theories by writing inclusively.
